summary: Overall UK risk remains Elevated. External geopolitical drivers continue to dominate the risk picture via energy and supply-chain exposure, with Brent still high (around $104/bbl on the latest reference quote) and ongoing disruption risk around key maritime routes. Domestically, acute natural-hazard signals are limited (no current Met Office UK weather warnings), but there is continued localised flood risk (one active flood warning and multiple flood alerts) and near-term service disruption risk from confirmed resident doctors’ strike action in England.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Energy-price sensitivity remains the primary macro risk, but the latest reference quote shows Brent lower than the prior update while UK inflation remains above target on the most recent ONS data.
drivers: Trading Economics: Brent crude 104.15 USD/bbl (31 March reference update), still high but lower than prior dashboard reading
ONS: CPI 3.0% y/y and CPIH 3.2% y/y (12 months to February 2026)
GBP/USD around 1.325 (Xe mid-market quote) and FTSE 100 reference level positive on the cited quote page (no dislocation signal in these reference sources)

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: The wider security environment remains stressed by ongoing Russia–Ukraine conflict dynamics and Middle East tensions with potential spillovers to energy markets and maritime routes; the UK terrorism threat level remains at a raised baseline.
drivers: MI5 national terrorism threat level remains SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely)
Ongoing Russia–Ukraine conflict remains active, sustaining a heightened European security backdrop
Middle East instability continues to create spillover risk to global energy and shipping

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: UK governance remains stable; government messaging remains focused on mitigating cost-of-living pressures and energy-price exposure rather than emergency domestic political measures.
drivers: GOV.UK (1 April): package of measures framed as easing cost-of-living pressure (wages, energy bills, targeted support)
Energy price cap and fuel duty measures signalled as continuing mitigation tools through mid-2026
No new confirmed UK constitutional or governance crisis indicators in the cited official update

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: No current Met Office UK weather warnings are listed at the time of update, but flood risk remains present with one active flood warning and multiple flood alerts.
drivers: Met Office UK warnings page shows no current active UK weather warnings
Environment Agency flood service lists 1 flood warning and 15 flood alerts (localised disruption risk)
Multiple active groundwater/coastal alerts indicate continued localised vulnerability

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: Near-term disruption risk remains elevated due to confirmed multi-day resident doctors’ strike action in England; broader cost-of-living sensitivity persists despite announced mitigation measures.
drivers: BMA: resident doctors’ strike action in England scheduled from 7am 7 April to 6.59am 13 April
Government framing emphasises ongoing cost-of-living pressure and continued need for mitigation
Knock-on impacts to healthcare access and local service resilience remain plausible during sustained industrial action

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: Cyber risk to UK organisations remains elevated; current guidance continues to emphasise rapid remediation for high-risk vulnerabilities and stronger baseline controls (including faster patching expectations).
drivers: NCSC has recently highlighted active exploitation affecting widely used enterprise software (per prior dashboard reference)
Cyber Essentials requirements update for April 2026 tightens expectations (including 14-day installation for high/critical updates and broader MFA requirements)
Persistent ransomware/cybercrime background risk remains a credible threat to service continuity

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics reference quote shows Brent at 104.15 USD/bbl (31 March update), down ~3.02% day-on-day and up ~33.97% over the past month on that page.

indicators.name: GBP/USD
indicators.note: Xe displayed 1.00 GBP = 1.32497883 USD (mid-market rate at 14:08 UTC on the page).

indicators.name: FTSE 100 (UKX)
indicators.note: TradingView reference quote page displayed FTSE 100 Index at 9,696.74 GBP, up 0.44% over the past 24 hours on that page.

indicators.name: UK inflation (CPI/CPIH)
indicators.note: ONS inflation landing page shows CPI and CPIH annual rates for the 12 months to February 2026.

indicators.name: Flood status (England)
indicators.note: Environment Agency flood service lists one flood warning and fifteen flood alerts at time of reading.

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat level
indicators.note: MI5 indicates the UK national threat level from all forms of terrorism is SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely).

changes_since_last_update: Economic / Financial trend marked improving because the latest reference quote shows Brent lower than the prior dashboard reading, though overall level remains Elevated given continued energy-price sensitivity and inflation above target.
Flood counts updated (Environment Agency now shows 1 warning and 15 alerts) with no change to Natural Hazards status.
Market indicator reference quotes refreshed (Brent, GBP/USD, FTSE 100) using current cited pages; overall status held at Elevated.
priority_watch_items: Energy price and supply-route risk: monitor Brent and any escalation affecting oil/gas flows and maritime chokepoints.
NHS disruption (7–13 April): monitor operational impacts of resident doctors’ strike and any local critical-incident declarations.
Flooding: monitor whether Environment Agency flood warnings/alerts expand or escalate, especially groundwater/coastal areas.
Exploited vulnerabilities: prioritise rapid patching for high/critical issues and monitor NCSC advisories.
Cyber control tightening: track Cyber Essentials April 2026 requirement changes (MFA and faster patching expectations) for downstream compliance pressure.
source_notes: This update relies on publicly visible reference quote pages for market indicators; some pages may be cached or lag intraday moves.
ONS inflation rates taken from the ONS inflation and price indices landing page (latest month shown: February 2026).
Flood counts taken from the Environment Agency flood warnings/alerts service; Met Office warnings status taken from the Met Office UK warnings page.
Strike timing taken from the BMA press release; impacts may vary by trust and local contingency planning.
Cyber posture notes reflect baseline-control guidance and scheme updates (Cyber Essentials); incident attribution and sector impacts can be incomplete or lagging.
